title: Ars Longa - A Song Of Pilgrimage
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our hopes are wild imaginings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our schemes are airy castles,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet these, on earth, are lords and kings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we their slaves and vassals;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your dream, forsooth, of buoyant youth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Most ready to deceive is;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But age will own the bitter truth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hill of life with eager feet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We climbed in merry morning,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But on the downward track we meet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shades of twilight warning;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shadows gaunt they fall aslant,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And those who scaled Ben Nevis,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against the mole-hills toil and pant,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The obstacles that barrן¿½d our path
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We seldom quailן¿½d to dash on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In youth, for youth one motto hath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The will, the way must fashion.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those words, I wot, blood thick and hot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Too ready to believe is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But thin and cold our blood hath got,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½art is longן¿½, and ן¿½life is shortן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And man is slow at learning;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yet by divers dealings taught,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For divers follies yearning,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He owns at last, with grief downcast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (For man disposed to grieve is)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One adage old stands true and fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We journey, manhood, youth, and age,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The matron, and the maiden,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like pilgrims on a pilgrimage,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loins girded, heavy laden:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each pilgrim strong, who joins our throng,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Most eager to achieve is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Foredoomן¿½d ere long to swell the song,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At morn, with staff and sandal-shoon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We travel brisk and cheery,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But some have laid them down ere noon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all at eve are weary;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The noontide glows with no repose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bitter chill the eve is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The grasshopper a burden grows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The staff is snappן¿½d, the sandal frayן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The flint-stone galls and blisters,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our brotherן¿½s steps we cannot aid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah me! nor aid our sisterן¿½s:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The pit prepares its hidden snares,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The rock prepared to cleave is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We cry, in falling unawares,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! Wisdom, which we sought to win!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! Strength, in which we trusted!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! Glory, which we gloried in!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! puppets we adjusted!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On barren land our seed is sand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And torn the web we weave is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bruised reed hath pierced the hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ars longa, vita brevis.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We, too, ן¿½Jobן¿½s comfortersן¿½ have met,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With steps, like ours, unsteady,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They could not help themselves, and yet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To judge us they were ready;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lifeן¿½s path is trod at last, and God
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More ready to reprieve is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They know who rest beneath the sod,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mors gratum, vita brevis.ן¿½
meaning: About: Adam Lindsay Gordon was an Australian poet, jockey and politician.
